items.q: What is operations management concerned with?
items.correct: The design and control of the production process and business operations — converting inputs into outputs efficiently. It includes capacity, quality, supply chain, and process improvement.
items.misconception: Operations management is the same as general management.
items.random_wrong: Operations management is only about manufacturing factories.
items.why: Operations is a specialised function focused on the production process; it applies equally to services (hospitals, airlines). Students treat it as factory-only or as general management, missing the distinctive process-focused discipline.

items.q: What is Taylor's theory of scientific management?
items.correct: Workers are motivated primarily by money; efficiency is maximised by breaking tasks into standardised steps, training workers in 'the one best way', and using piece-rate pay.
items.misconception: Taylor said workers are motivated by recognition and teamwork.
items.random_wrong: Taylor invented the assembly line at Ford.
items.why: Students confuse Taylor with Mayo or Maslow. Taylor's view was that workers respond to pay incentives, not social or psychological factors — his theory is famously narrow and mechanistic.

items.correct: Cost advantages a firm gains as it grows — average cost per unit falls because fixed costs spread over more units, bulk discounts, and specialisation become possible.
items.misconception: Economies of scale means total costs decrease as the business grows.
items.random_wrong: Economies of scale means a business saves the economy money.
items.why: Total costs RISE with growth — it's AVERAGE cost per unit that falls. Students confuse total and average, missing the precise meaning of the concept.

items.q: Quality assurance is best described as:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Building processes that prevent defects. “Inspecting finished goods” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Six Sigma aims for defect rates of around:
items.why: Correct answer: 3.4 per million. “3.4 per hundred” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Six Sigma's target defect rate per million opportunities?
items.why: The real figure is 3.4. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
